channel.title: Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: Black Hole Thermodynamics
item.description:
In the early 1970s, Jacob Bekenstein proposed something strange: black holes have entropy. Stephen Hawking then showed something stranger: black holes have temperature.

¶The problem Bekenstein saw

Suppose you have a box of hot gas – high entropy. You throw it into a black hole. The gas is gone. If the black hole has no entropy, you have just reduced the total entropy of the universe. The second law of thermodynamics forbids this.

item.title: The Information Paradox
item.description:
Hawking radiation creates a problem. If a black hole evaporates completely, what happens to the information about everything that fell in?

¶The setup

Quantum mechanics is unitary. This means the evolution of a quantum system is reversible: given the final state, you can in principle reconstruct the initial state. No information is ever truly lost.

But Hawking radiation appears to be exactly thermal – random noise determined only by the black hole’s mass, charge, and angular momentum. It carries no detailed information about the specific matter that fell in. A black hole formed from a collapsing star and a black hole formed from an equivalent mass of encyclopedias would emit the same radiation. If the black hole evaporates completely, the detailed information about its contents is gone.

item.title: What Is a Black Hole?
item.description:
Take a star and compress it. Make it smaller and smaller, denser and denser. At some point, the gravitational pull at the surface becomes so strong that light itself cannot escape. You have made a black hole.

¶The geometry

A black hole is not an object in the usual sense. It is a region of spacetime where gravity – or more precisely, the curvature of spacetime – is so extreme that all paths lead inward. The boundary of this region is the event horizon: the surface at which the escape velocity equals the speed of light.

item.title: Black Holes as Information-Theoretic Stability Optimizers
item.description:
¶Abstract

We propose that black holes can be modeled as information-theoretic stability optimizers: physical systems that maximize entropy while minimizing divergence between interior and exterior information distributions. Using the definition of stability reward as a function of coupling between systems (see Information-Theoretic Stability as Reward Function), we reformulate the laws of black-hole thermodynamics as expressions of informational coupling across the horizon. The event horizon is interpreted as a stability boundary where entropy flux across the surface balances the internal rate of informational compression. This perspective unifies the thermodynamic, holographic, and information-geometric descriptions of black holes and suggests a general stability principle underlying gravitational dynamics.
